meta.subject: Physics
meta.chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

notes.title: Energy Bands
points: Inside a crystal, each electron has a unique position and no two electrons see exactly the same pattern of surrounding charges.
Valence Band: The energy band which includes the energy levels of the valence electrons.
Conduction Band: The energy band above the valence band. Electrons here are free to move.
Energy Gap (Eg): The separation between the top of the valence band and the bottom of the conduction band.

notes.title: Classification based on Energy Bands
notes.caption: (a) Metals: Overlapping bands or no gap. (b) Semiconductors: Small gap (Eg < 3 eV). (c) Insulators: Large gap (Eg > 3 eV).

notes.title: Intrinsic Semiconductor
points: Pure semiconductors (Si, Ge) free of impurities.
At T = 0 K, they behave as perfect insulators.
At T > 0 K, thermal energy excites some electrons from Valence Band to Conduction Band, creating equal numbers of electrons (ne) and holes (nh).

notes.label: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration
conditions: ni is the intrinsic carrier concentration.

notes.title: Extrinsic Semiconductor
points: n-type: Doped with Pentavalent impurity (As, Sb, P). Majority carriers: Electrons. Donor energy level lies just below the conduction band.
p-type: Doped with Trivalent impurity (In, B, Al). Majority carriers: Holes. Acceptor energy level lies just above the valence band.

notes.title: Energy Bands in Extrinsic Semiconductors
notes.caption: Shows Donor level (Ed) near Conduction Band for n-type, and Acceptor level (Ea) near Valence Band for p-type.

notes.label: Law of Mass Action
conditions: Valid for both intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors in thermal equilibrium.

notes.title: p-n Junction Formation
points: Diffusion: Holes diffuse from p-side to n-side; Electrons diffuse from n-side to p-side due to concentration gradient.
Depletion Region: A region near the junction devoid of free charge carriers, containing only immobile ions.
Barrier Potential: An electric field E is developed across the depletion region (n-side positive, p-side negative) which opposes further diffusion.

notes.title: p-n Junction and Barrier Potential
notes.caption: Depletion region width and potential barrier V0 across the junction.

notes.title: Semiconductor Diode Biasing
points: Forward Bias: p-side connected to Positive, n-side to Negative. Barrier height decreases. Diffusion current dominates. Width of depletion layer decreases.
Reverse Bias: p-side connected to Negative, n-side to Positive. Barrier height increases. Drift current dominates (very small). Width of depletion layer increases.

notes.title: V-I Characteristics of p-n Junction Diode
notes.caption: Exponential rise in forward bias (after threshold voltage); small saturation current in reverse bias.

notes.label: Diode Current Equation
conditions: V is positive for forward bias, negative for reverse.

notes.title: Half-Wave Rectifier
assumptions: Transforms AC input to pulsating DC.

result.direction: Current flows only during the positive half-cycle of input AC.

notes.title: Half-Wave Rectifier Circuit and Waveform
notes.caption: Diode conducts only when A is positive with respect to B.

notes.title: Full-Wave Rectifier
assumptions: Uses two diodes (D1, D2) with a center-tapped transformer.

result.direction: D1 conducts in positive half-cycle, D2 conducts in negative half-cycle. Current direction in load RL is same.

notes.title: Full-Wave Rectifier Circuit and Waveform
notes.caption: Center-tap transformer allows current through load in same direction for both half cycles.

ncert_golden_lines: At absolute zero, a pure semiconductor behaves as an insulator.
Carbon, silicon and germanium have the same lattice structure and have 4 valence electrons.
In an intrinsic semiconductor, the number of free electrons, ne is equal to the number of holes, nh.
The resistivity of a semiconductor decreases with temperature (negative temperature coefficient of resistance).
In forward bias, the width of the depletion layer decreases and the barrier height is reduced.
In reverse bias, the diode acts like an open switch.
A rectifier is a device which converts alternating current (ac) into direct current (dc).

diagram_descriptors.detailed_description: (a) Metals: Overlapping bands or no gap. (b) Semiconductors: Small gap (Eg < 3 eV). (c) Insulators: Large gap (Eg > 3 eV).
Shows Donor level (Ed) near Conduction Band for n-type, and Acceptor level (Ea) near Valence Band for p-type.
Depletion region width and potential barrier V0 across the junction.
